standard_description: Classify two-dimensional figures based on the presence or absence of parallel or perpendicular lines, or the presence or absence of angles of a specified size. Recognize right triangles as a category, and identify right triangles.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
Arya draws a figure that is a polygon. Which of the following is true about the figure? There are
three
correct answers.
Problem 2
When is a polygon also a quadrilateral?

Task
###IMAGE0###
Say what a trapezoid is in your own words. Compare your definition with a partner.
Is this parallelogram a trapezoid according to your definition? Explain.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
Someone sorted some shapes as shown below.
###IMAGE0###
a.   What is similar about the two groups of figures? What term(s) can we use to describe all of the shapes?
b.   What is different about the two groups of shapes?
Problem 2
Decide whether each of the following statements are true or false. If it is false, rewrite the statement so that it is true.
a.   A trapezoid is always a quadrilateral.
b.   A quadrilateral is never a trapezoid.

List one attribute that each of the following pairs of shapes have in common. There may be more than one correct answer.
a.   Square and rhombus
b.   Square and rectangle
c.   Square and parallelogram
d.   Square and trapezoid
e.   Square and quadrilateral

Problem 1
Draw a right scalene triangle.
Problem 2
Draw an acute isosceles triangle.
Problem 3
True or false: all triangles have at least 2 acute angles.

Identify whether the following statements are true or false.
a.   A parallelogram is always a trapezoid. T / F
b.   A quadrilateral is always a parallelogram. T / F
c.   A parallelogram is always a polygon. T / F

Problem 1
For each figure in the table below, determine whether each statement describes the figure. If so, place an X in the box. You may mark more than one box for each figure.
###TABLE0###
Problem 2
Based on the properties identified in the table above and any other properties you can identify, name each shape, being as specific as possible. Write the name of each shape below.
Shape A: ______________
Shape B: ______________
Shape C: ______________

Problem 1
A student sorted a set of shapes into the following 2 categories:
###IMAGE0###
How were the shapes sorted?
Problem 2
Sort the polygons from Anchor Task #1 (cut out from
Polygons Template
) however you’d like. You can create as many groups as you’d like.
Problem 3
a.   Draw a polygon that is a triangle. Explain or show how you know it is a polygon and a triangle.
b.   Draw a polygon that is not a triangle. Explain or show how you know it is a polygon but not a triangle.
c.   Jamshid says that it is impossible to draw a triangle that is not a polygon. Do you agree with him? Explain or show your reasoning.

Problem 1
Draw lines to match each triangle below with one term that describes its sides and one term that describes its angles.
###TABLE0###
Problem 2
Explain how you determined how to classify triangle C.

Task
Draw at least two examples and two non-examples of each of the quadrilaterals defined below.
Parallelogram:  A quadrilateral with 2 pairs of parallel sides.
Rectangle:  A parallelogram with 4 right angles.
Rhombus: A parallelogram with 4 sides with equal length.
I am a shape that is a parallelogram, a rectangle, and at the same time a rhombus. What shape am I?  Draw a diagram of what I look like.  Use the vocabulary words and their definitions given in part (a) to explain what shape I am.

Problem 1
###TABLE0###
Based on this sort, say what a parallelogram is in your own words. Compare your definition with a partner.
Problem 2
Decide whether the following statements are always, sometimes, or never true.
a.   A polygon is a parallelogram.
b.   A parallelogram is a polygon.

Problem 1
Draw a trapezoid that has no right angles on the following grid.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Draw a shape that has four right angles and four equal sides on the following grid.
###IMAGE1###
Problem 3
What is the mathematical name for the shape you drew in Problem 2? Be as specific as possible.

Problem 1
Act 1: Watch the video:
Investigating Quadrilaterals
.
a.   What do you notice? What do you wonder?
b.   How is this person sorting the cards each time?
Problem 2
Act 2: Use the following information to solve.
###TABLE0###
Problem 3
Act 3: Reveal the answer.
The shapes were first sorted according to whether they had at least one set of parallel sides or not.
The shapes were then sorted according to whether they had two pairs of parallel sides or not.
Was your answer reasonable? Why or why not?
###TABLE1###
Problem 4
Act 4 (the sequel): Sort the quadrilaterals from
Template: Quadrilaterals
in a different way from how they were sorted in the three-act task above.

Task
In the figure, $ABCD$ is a rectangle and $\angle CAD = 31^{\circ}$. Find $\angle BAC$.
###IMAGE0###

Task
Look at each figure. Read each of the descriptions. Place an X in the box if it appears to describe the figure pictured.
###TABLE0###
What is true of all the figures?
All of the figures above are rectangles. Ring the rectangles below. Using what you know that is true of all rectangles to help you.
###IMAGE0###
Choose one figure you did not ring. Explain why it is not a rectangle.
Look at each figure. Read each of the attributes. Place an X in the box if it appears to be an attribute of the figure pictured.
###IMAGE1###
What is true of all the figures?
All of the figures above are parallelograms. Ring all the parallelograms below. Using what you know that is true of all parallelograms to help you.
###IMAGE2###
Choose one figure you did not ring. Explain why it is not a parallelogram.

metadata.title: Defining Attributes of Rectangles and Parallelograms

problems.text:
Problem 1
For each statement below, circle whether it is always true, sometimes true, or never true.
a.   A parallelogram is a rhombus.                 Always     Sometimes     Never
b.   A rhombus is a parallelogram.                 Always     Sometimes     Never
Problem 2
Which of the following describes the shape below? There are
three
correct answers.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
What would have to be true of the shape in #2 in order for it to belong in all four shape categories (A, B, C, and D)?

Problem 1
Draw four points on the grid below so that when connected they form a quadrilateral. Use your straightedge to connect the four points to form a quadrilateral.
###IMAGE0###
How can you classify your quadrilateral?
What attributes did you look at to classify the quadrilateral?
What tools did you use to help draw your quadrilateral and classify it?
Problem 2
Draw an example of each of the following shapes or explain why it’s not possible to draw one.
Quadrilateral with just one pair of parallel sides
Quadrilateral with four equal sides
Quadrilateral with 3 obtuse angles

Task
Which of the polygons are right triangles? Choose a measuring tool to help you determine this.
###IMAGE0###
###TABLE0###
For each right triangle, identify the right angle by marking it with a small square. Explain why the other polygons are not right triangles.

Problem 1
a.   Sort the shapes (cut out from
Triangles Template
) into as many categories as you’d like. Then explain how you sorted them.
b.   Sort the shapes (cut out from
Triangles Template
) in another way. Then explain how you sorted them.
Problem 2
###IMAGE0###
a.   What are the characteristics shared by shapes within circle A? Within circle B? Within circle C? Double-check to make sure that any shapes that have that characteristic are contained within the circle and any shapes that don't lie outside of the circle.
b.   Where would you place a rectangle that does not have four sides of the same length? Why?
c.   What kind of shape could go in the center of this Venn Diagram? Be specific about its properties. If there could not be a shape that belongs in the center, justify your reasoning.

Problem 1
Which of the following best describes the triangle below?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Which of the following statements is true about every scalene obtuse triangle?

Problem 1
Sort the shapes (cut out from
Template: Triangles
) into as many categories as you’d like. Then explain how you sorted them.
Sort the shapes (cut out from
Template: Triangles
) in another way. Then explain how you sorted them.
Problem 2
Fill out the following table based on your triangles (
Template: Triangles
).
###TABLE0###

Problem 1
Draw three points on the grid below so that when connected they form a triangle. Use your straightedge to connect the three points to form a triangle.
###IMAGE0###
How can you classify your triangle?
What attributes did you look at to classify the triangle?
What tools did you use to help you draw your triangle and classify it?
Problem 2
Draw an example of each of the following shapes or explain why it’s not possible to draw one.
Right isosceles triangle
Acute scalene triangle
Obtuse equilateral triangle

Problem 1
a.   Sort the shapes (cut out from
Parallelograms Template
) into as many categories as you’d like. Then explain how you sorted them.
b.   Sort the shapes (cut out from
Parallelograms Template
) in another way. Then explain how you sorted them.
Problem 2
Add to the Venn diagram from Lesson 12 so that it represents all of the categories of quadrilaterals we’ve encountered so far.

Problem 4
Pre-unit
Which of the triangles are right triangles?
###IMAGE0###

Task
In the figure, $ABCD$ is a rectangle and $\angle CAD = 31^{\circ}$. Find $\angle BAC$.
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
###IMAGE0###
Here is our Venn diagram from yesterday’s lesson, with an added category label of “squares.” How would you define a
square
?
Problem 2
Do you agree or disagree with the following statements? Explain.
a.   All squares are rectangles. Rectangles have four right angles. Therefore, all squares have four right angles.
b.   No trapezoids are squares. Squares have four sides of equal length. Therefore, no trapezoids have four sides of equal length.

Problem 1
What attributes do quadrilaterals and trapezoids share?
Problem 2
What attributes do trapezoids have that not all quadrilaterals have?

IM Commentary
The purpose of this task is to give 4th grade students a problem involving an unknown
quantity that has a clear visual representation. Students must understand that the four
ABCD
∠CAD = 31∘
∠BAC
1
Illustrative 
Mathematics

interior angles of a rectangle are all right angles (4.G.2) and that right angles have a
measure of 
 and that angle measure is additive (4.MD.7). In a teaching scenario,
students may be allowed to verify the computations using a protractor to measure the
angles. However, care should be taken beforehand to ensure that the measurements
of the printed figure match the stated measurements.
The task may also be viewed as preparation for later work when, in 6th grade, students
are introduced to algebraic expressions. In that context, unknown angle problems will
use variables to label missing angles, and students will write and solve equations to
find the missing angle measures.
This task includes an experimental GeoGebra worksheet, with the intent that
instructors might use it to more interactively demonstrate the relevant content
material. The file should be considered a draft version, and feedback on it in the
comment section is highly encouraged, both in terms of suggestions for improvement
and for ideas on using it effectively. The file can be run via the free online application
GeoGebra, or run locally if GeoGebra has been installed on a computer.
This applet allows the teacher to first enter the any angle. After setting the angle, the
problem appears and can be given to the student.

4.G What shape am I?
Alignments to Content Standards:  4.G.A.2
Task
a. Draw at least two examples and two non-examples of each of the quadrilaterals
defined below.
Parallelogram: A quadrilateral with 2 pairs of parallel sides.
Rectangle: A parallelogram with 4 right angles.
Rhombus: A parallelogram with 4 sides with equal length.
b. I am a shape that is a parallelogram, a rectangle, and at the same time a rhombus.
What shape am I? Draw a diagram of what I look like. Use the vocabulary words and
their definitions given in part (a) to explain what shape I am.
IM Commentary
This task builds on the geometric knowledge that students developed in 2nd grade
(2.G.1). In this task, students ultimately use the definitions they are given for three
types of quadrilaterals and what they know about parallel sides to identify that a
square fits all the definitions and explain why. By drawing examples and non-examples
for each kind of shape, students get an opportunity to explore their individual
properties before relating their knowledge of all three to the defining attributes of a
square.
Students should be encouraged to work in small groups and use the correct vocabulary
as they talk together about classifying shapes. Students should take care to draw their
figures with a straight-edge so that they can draw precise shapes. It is a good idea to
have a whole-group discussion about this task to be sure that students understand the
1
Illustrative 
Mathematics

relationship between these different quadrilaterals.
For students who need more scaffolding, the teacher can provide a bank of shapes for
them to sort as “examples” and “non-examples” in part (a). These can be cut-outs or
graphics on the page, depending on the needs of the students. A graphic organizer
would help students to summarize their thinking from this task.
Edit this solution
Solution
a. There are many possible examples and non-examples; the figure below shows a few.
b. A square has two pairs of parallel sides, therefore it is a parallelogram; in the figure,
we see that 
 is parallel to 
 and 
 is parallel to 
. A square also has 4 right
angles, so therefore it is a rectangle. A square also has 4 congruent sides, therefore it is
a rhombus.

rectangles and rhombuses are also parallelograms, we can show the relationship
between these figures with this Venn diagram:

khan_exercises.title: Identify parallel and perpendicular lines
khan_exercises.description: Recognize parallel and perpendicular lines in geometric figures and pictures.
topic_path: Module 4: Angle measure and plane figures

items.question_content:
**Which of these figures shows parallel lines?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Parallel lines}}$ are always the same distance apart from each other — no matter how far we extend them, they will never meet.

[[☃ image 1]]

Which figure shows *parallel lines*?
This figure shows *parallel* lines:

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![Two lines that meet at right angles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/86975c7968fc3e94825df93eb44b1b0d15a8f14d)
![Two lines that are the same distance apart from each other and will never intersect.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8a8adf8dbb35ca7b96223a7e4a8a6e867f692cfe)
![Two lines that intersect each other.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4578b30eaf94525a86de3a95953e08df5dda586d)

choices.content: Parallel lines
Perpendicular lines

items.question_content:
Look at the map below, then answer the question based on the appearance of the lines on the map. 

**Which statement about Main Street and Pine Street is correct? **

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Parallel lines}}$ are always the same distance apart from each other — no matter how far we extend them, they will never meet.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenE{\text{Perpendicular lines}}$ are lines that meet at right angles.  



[[☃ image 2]]

We can compare these to the lines formed by Main Street and Pine Street.

Looking at the map, we can see that Main Street and Pine Street intersect, or meet at an angle. 

Parallel lines never meet, no matter how far we extend them, so the two streets are **not** parallel. 



[[☃ image 1]]

We can also see that Main Street and Pine Street do **not** meet at a right angle, so the two streets are not perpendicular.
Main Street is neither parallel nor perpendicular to Pine Street.

options.alt: A street map. Holly Street and Pine Street are the same distance apart from each other and will never meet. Main Street intersects both Holly Street and Pine Street at an angle to both. Alta Street intersects Holly Street and Pine Street at right angles to both.

choices.content: Main Street is parallel to Pine Street.
Main Street is perpendicular to Pine Street.
Main Street is neither parallel nor perpendicular to Pine Street.

items.question_content:
Look at the map below, then answer the question based on the appearance of the lines on the map. 

**Which statement about $2\text{nd}$ Street and Market Street is correct? **

[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Parallel lines}}$ are always the same distance apart from each other — no matter how far we extend them, they will never meet.

[[☃ image 1]]


$\greenE{\text{Perpendicular lines}}$ are lines that meet at right angles.  

[[☃ image 2]]

We can compare these to the lines formed by $2\text{nd}$ Street and Market Street.

Looking at the map, we can see that $2\text{nd}$ Street and Market Street intersect, or meet at a right angle. 

[[☃ image 1]]
$2\text{nd}$ Street is perpendicular to Market Street.

options.alt: A street map. 1st Street and 2nd Street are the same distance apart from each other and will never meet. King Street intersects both 1st Street and 2nd Street at an angle to both. Market Street intersects 1st Street and 2nd Street at right angles to both.

choices.content: $2\text{nd}$ Street is parallel to Market Street.
$2\text{nd}$ Street is perpendicular to Market Street.
$2\text{nd}$ Street is neither parallel nor perpendicular to Market Street.

items.question_content:
Look at the map below, then answer the question based on the appearance of the lines on the map. 

**Which statement about Robin Street and Owl Street is correct? **



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\purpleD{\text{Parallel lines}}$ are always the same distance apart from each other — no matter how far we extend them, they will never meet.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenE{\text{Perpendicular lines}}$ are lines that meet at right angles.  


[[☃ image 2]]

We can compare these to the lines formed by Robin Street and Owl Street.

Looking at the map, we can see that Robin Street and Owl Street are always the same distance apart from each other — no matter how far we extend them, they will never meet.


[[☃ image 1]]
Robin Street is parallel to Owl Street.

options.alt: A street map. Robin Street and Owl Street are the same distance apart from each other and will never meet. Dove Street intersects both Robin Street and Owl Street at an angle to both. Red Street intersects Robin Street and Owl Street at an angle to both.

choices.content: Robin Street is parallel to Owl Street.
Robin Street is perpendicular to Owl Street.
Robin Street is neither parallel nor perpendicular to Owl Street.

items.question_content:
Look at the flag of Trinidad and Tobago below, then answer the question based on the appearance of the lines on the flag. 

**Which statement about the two white stripes in the flag of Trinidad and Tobago is correct? **




[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Below are examples of $\greenC{\text{a pair of perpendicular lines}}$ and $\purpleC{\text{a pair of parallel lines}}$.  


[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ image 2]]

We can compare these to the white stripes in the flag of Trinidad and Tobago.

Looking at the flag, we can see that the white stripes do not meet to form angles. 

Perpendicular lines meet to form $90^\circ$ angles, so the white stripes are **not** perpendicular. 




[[☃ image 1]]

We can also see that the white stripes are always the same distance apart and do not meet.
The white stripes in the flag of Trinidad and Tobago are parallel to each other.

options.alt: The Trinidad and Tobago flag is red with a thick diagonal black stripe between two thin diagonal white stripes. Two white stripes are the same distance apart from each other and will never meet.

choices.content: The white stripes are parallel to each other.
The white stripes are perpendicular to each other.
The white stripes are neither parallel nor perpendicular to each other.

items.question_content:
Look at the flag of Aruba below, then answer the question based on the appearance of the lines on the flag. 

**Which statement about the two yellow stripes in the flag of Aruba is correct? **


[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Below are examples of $\greenC{\text{a pair of perpendicular lines}}$ and $\purpleC{\text{a pair of parallel lines}}$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]


We can compare these to the yellow stripes in the flag of Aruba.

Looking at the flag, we can see that the yellow stripes do not meet to form angles. 

Perpendicular lines meet to form $90^\circ$ angles, so the yellow stripes are **not** perpendicular. 

[[☃ image 1]]



We can also see that the yellow stripes are always the same distance apart and do not meet.
The yellow stripes in the flag of Aruba are parallel to each other.

options.alt: The Aruba flag is blue with a red 4-point star outlined in white in the top left corner. Two yellow lines near the bottom are the same distance apart from each other and will never meet.

choices.content: The yellow stripes are parallel to each other.
The yellow stripes are perpendicular to each other.
The yellow stripes are neither parallel nor perpendicular to each other.

items.question_content:
Look at the flag of Sweden below, then answer the question based on the appearance of the lines on the flag. 

**Which statement about the two yellow stripes in the Swedish flag is correct? **


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Below are examples of $\greenC{\text{a pair of perpendicular lines}}$ and $\purpleC{\text{a pair of parallel lines}}$.  


[[☃ image 1]]



[[☃ image 2]]

We can compare these to the yellow stripes in the flag of Sweden.

Looking at the flag, we can see that the yellow stripes intersect. 

Parallel lines never meet, no matter how far they are extended, so the two yellow stripes are **not** parallel. 



[[☃ image 1]]

We can also see that the yellow stripes meet at $90^\circ$ angles.
The two yellow stripes in the Swedish flag are perpendicular to each other.

options.alt: The flag of Sweden is blue with a yellow cross formed by two stripes. A vertical yellow stripe intersects with a horizontal yellow stripe at a right angle.

choices.content: The two yellow stripes in the Swedish flag are parallel to each other.
The two yellow stripes in the Swedish flag are perpendicular to each other.
The two yellow stripes in the Swedish flag are neither parallel nor perpendicular to each other.
